Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict study routine. I'd study when I felt like it, maybe 15 hours a week spread across random times. Sometimes I'd get into a groove late at night, other times I'd study between my extracurricular activities. I know it sounds disorganized, but it worked for me because I don't stress about these things too much.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'm not the type to block out specific study hours every week. When deadlines were coming up, I'd put in more effort, but otherwise I kind of just went with the flow. My other activities and social life often took priority, and I'd fit studying around everything else.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered there was something due or when I got a notification. Sometimes I'd log in just to browse and see what was happening in the discussion forums. It wasn't like a daily habit or anything—more like when something caught my attention or I knew I had to check on an assignment.
Q4: For a while, yeah, it became kind of routine to check in during my morning coffee. I'd scroll through the course updates and see if anything new was posted. It happened naturally because I was already on my laptop anyway. But that routine came and went depending on how busy I was with other things.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually pretty useful because I could see how other students were thinking about the material. I also liked the video lectures when I actually watched them—they helped break things down. Reading materials were good too since I'm more of a read-write learner, so I could take notes and process things at my own pace.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the optional readings and some of the supplementary videos. If it wasn't going to be on an exam or directly tied to an assignment, I usually skipped it. I just didn't see the point in doing extra work when I had so many other things going on in my life.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to what I needed to pass or if it was genuinely interesting. I'm pretty good at filtering out busy work. If something seemed like it would actually help me understand the material or boost my grade, I'd engage with it. Otherwise, I'd move on.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions rather than obligations. I'd look at them and think, 'that's nice, but do I really need this?' Required activities had a different weight because I knew they'd affect my grade. I completed what I had to, but optional stuff was easy to let slide without any guilt.
Q9: Oh yeah, definitely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and readings at the start of the semester and studied from those on my tablet. I also used my own notes and sometimes looked things up on YouTube or other websites. The LMS was just one piece of how I actually learned the material.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded articles without ever touching the LMS. I'd also discuss concepts with friends over coffee, which was actually really helpful for understanding things. None of that showed up anywhere in my course activity logs.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides or readings once and then never open them again on the LMS. I'd study from the files on my laptop, annotate them, and review them multiple times offline. The system would only show that one download, but I probably looked at those materials a dozen times.
Q12: There were a few weeks where I just couldn't be bothered, honestly. Nothing dramatic was happening—I just felt disconnected from the course content. My extracurriculars were demanding more of my attention, and the course material felt dry during certain modules. But I bounced back pretty easily.
Q13: Not really in a stressful way. I have pretty low stress levels overall, so even when I had competing responsibilities, I didn't panic. I just prioritized what seemed most urgent or interesting. The LMS usage probably dipped during busy weeks, but I didn't feel overwhelmed by it.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open a video or reading and just zone out completely. I was technically 'accessing' the material, but my mind was somewhere else. I'd have to go back later and actually engage with it properly. Those initial views were basically meaningless in terms of actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments pretty regularly, even important ones. I knew I should do them earlier, but I just... didn't feel like it. I have high resilience, so I trusted myself to pull through at the last minute. It's not ideal, but it's how I've always operated and I still passed.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator for using the LMS. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached and actually complete work. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They gave me the structure I wasn't giving myself.
Q17: Feedback was nice when I got it, and it did make me feel more connected to the course. If I got positive feedback, I'd feel good and maybe engage a bit more. But honestly, it didn't drastically change my behavior. I appreciated it without letting it drive my actions.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which suited me in some ways but also made it easy to slack off. When things were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I performed better. The modules that were more open-ended were the ones where I drifted the most.
Q19: Having clear expectations and organized modules made engagement easier. When I had to hunt around for what was due or important, I'd sometimes just give up and do the minimum. The discussion forums were well-designed though, and I actually enjoyed participating in those.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—browsing, clicking around, maybe even genuinely interested, but not following through on the actual work. High activity doesn't mean high productivity. You can spend hours on the LMS without actually producing anything meaningful.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. I didn't log in constantly, but I still did decently on exams because I studied in my own way offline. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything. Some people are just efficient learners who don't need to be constantly clicking around online.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which is useful for tracking basic engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens outside the platform. They can't measure understanding, interest, or offline study habits.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. For me, engagement is when I'm mentally present and curious, even if I'm not always consistent about it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I discussed course concepts with friends, how many times I reviewed downloaded materials, or the mindfulness practices that kept me feeling positive throughout the semester. It also wouldn't capture that I was genuinely happy and interested in learning, just not in a way that translated to constant online activity.
